title: Ankara Summit Aftermath: Trump Pledges CAATSA Lift, S-400 Exit Deal Emerges
subhead: The 2026 NATO Ankara Summit closed with Trump vowing to lift CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and dangling F-35s for Erdogan, while details emerge of a plan to transfer Russian S-400s to a Gulf state — and Turkey revealed it blocked a US-backed Kurdish invasion of Iran during the war.

sections.heading: Trump Pledges CAATSA Lift, S-400 Transfer to Gulf Under Discussion
sections.body:
President Trump announced at the NATO Ankara Summit that the US 'will lift CAATSA sanctions' on Turkey, a move that would remove penalties imposed over Ankara's 2019 purchase of Russian S-400 air defense systems. However, the path to Turkey's return to the F-35 program remains complicated.

According to Hürriyet columnist Hande Fırat, Turkey and the US are discussing transferring the S-400s to a Persian Gulf country — with Turkish media naming the UAE as a likely buyer. Options that don't meet US legal requirements include deactivating the systems, dismantling launch components, or storing them in Turkey under US monitoring. The physical removal of the S-400s from Turkish territory appears to be the formula Washington requires.

Even a CAATSA lift won't automatically restore F-35 access. Congress embedded separate provisions in the FY2021 NDAA restricting F-35 deliveries to Turkey, meaning the White House will need to convince Congress to amend or exempt Turkey from those restrictions — a separate political and legal process.

Trump said he promised Erdogan 'five' F-35s, though Erdogan added: 'I know that Mr. Trump always keeps his promises.' On the F-35 specifically, Trump was more cautious, saying an assessment would be conducted and no decision had yet been reached.

Separately, the State Department has already notified Congress of a planned sale of engines worth over $700 million for Turkey's indigenous KAAN fighter jet, indicating a broader thaw in defense trade.
sections.whyItMatters: The CAATSA lift and potential S-400 transfer represent the most significant US-Turkey defense breakthrough in years — but F-35 delivery still requires a congressional fight that Israel and Greece will lobby hard against.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Defense Spending, Counter-Drone Push, Ukraine Support
sections.body:
The July 7–8 NATO Summit in Ankara produced a summit declaration announcing more than $50 billion in new procurements and expanded collective manufacturing capacity. Secretary General Rutte reported that member states had spent $37 billion in one year on defense, calling it 'money well spent' and urging a defense industry 'revolution.'

NATO also announced a $40 billion investment in counter-drone capabilities over five years, acknowledging that drones have 'fundamentally altered the character of modern warfare' and become a 'decisive factor on the battlefield.'

On spending targets, Reuters reported that only five of 32 members are projected to meet the 3.5% core defense GDP target in 2026: Lithuania (5.33%), Estonia (5.1%), Latvia (4.92%), Poland (4.68%), and Greece (3.65%). Albania (1.48%), Slovenia (1.57%), and Czechia (1.86%) remain below even the old 2% floor.

Ukraine's Zelenskyy addressed the NATO-Ukraine Council, pressing again for Alliance membership and additional air defense. Trump told Zelenskyy the US would grant Ukraine a production license for Patriot missile interceptors — though companies behind the system had not yet been informed. The Czech Republic said it would not participate in a €70 billion military assistance package for Ukraine.

Erdogan gave every NATO leader a Sarsılmaz SR38 revolver as a parting gift — a promotional gesture for Turkey's defense industry that bemused several recipients.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ankara Summit confirmed Turkey's repositioning at the center of NATO — but the spending gap between frontline states and southern/western members remains wide, and the $40B counter-drone fund signals that drone warfare is now a core Alliance priority.

sections.heading: Turkey Blocked US-Backed Kurdish Invasion of Iran During the War
sections.body:
Israel's i24News reported that Turkey thwarted a planned US- and Israeli-backed Kurdish military incursion into Iran during the US-Iran war earlier this year. According to Israeli sources, Kurdish militias had been trained and armed by US intelligence for months, with weapons delivered a week before the planned attack — but the operation was vetoed by Washington after Erdogan pressured the US to halt it.

Israeli officials accused US Vice President JD Vance of leaking details of the operation to Ankara, prompting Turkish officials to move immediately to shut it down.

CNN reported in March that the CIA had been arming Kurdish groups along the Iran-Iraq border for months before the war began. Reuters also reported that Israeli intelligence had been in contact with Kurdish militias on the Iran-Iraq border for a year before the conflict.

Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan warned US Secretary of State Marco Rubio in March against supporting any Kurdish operation in Iran. A collapsed Iranian state would create a new security threat on Turkey's southeastern border — a red line for Ankara regardless of its relations with Tehran.
sections.whyItMatters: This reveals the depth of Turkey's opposition to Kurdish militarization anywhere near its borders — even when backed by its own NATO ally. It also exposes a rift between Israel and Turkey: Israeli officials saw the Kurdish operation as integral to regime change in Iran, while Ankara treated it as an existential threat.

sections.heading: Israel-Turkey Tensions Escalate: 'Chokehold' Warnings and Strategic Rivalry
sections.body:
Israeli-Turkish tensions deepened significantly this week. Israeli Diaspora Affairs Minister Amichai Chikli declared that 'a scenario in which Turkey attacks Israel is entirely possible,' and Israel's Channel 12 reported that the security establishment warned the government about Turkey's growing influence in Syria, Lebanon, and the broader Middle East — claiming Ankara is building a 'chokehold' around Israel.

Chikli described the new strategic landscape: 'The era of the Shiite empire of Iran, Assad's Syria, and Hezbollah, is over. The new axis is the Muslim Brotherhood axis of Turkey, Syria, and Qatar.' An AI-generated video circulating in Israel showed Erdogan among Israel's enemies, with his 'number being called' in a waiting room.

Foreign Minister Fidan pushed back, telling CNN Turk: 'Israel is not just my problem; Israel is the world's problem.'

A Euro2day analysis noted that deeper Turkish integration into NATO also serves as an institutional safeguard against uncontrolled confrontation with Israel — the more embedded Turkey is in Alliance structures, the harder it is for a non-member to treat it as a second-tier player.
sections.whyItMatters: The Israel-Turkey rivalry is shifting from rhetorical hostility to strategic confrontation over Syria, Lebanon, and the Eastern Mediterranean — with both sides now describing the other as a primary threat.

sections.heading: Turkey-Iraq Extend Kirkuk-Ceyhan Pipeline Deal for 12 Months
sections.body:
Turkish Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar announced that Turkey and Iraq will sign a 12-month extension of the agreement governing the Iraq-Turkey Crude Oil Pipeline, preventing a July 27 expiration that would have cut off one of Baghdad's few remaining viable export routes.

The pipeline, running from Kirkuk to the Mediterranean terminal at Ceyhan, has become indispensable since the Strait of Hormuz was effectively shut down during the Iran war. With Iraq's southern exports trapped in the Gulf, the Ceyhan route transformed from a secondary option into an economic lifeline — oil accounts for roughly 90% of Iraqi government revenue.

The pipeline has a turbulent history: it was offline for over two years after an arbitration court ordered Turkey to pay Baghdad $1.5 billion over unauthorized Kurdish oil exports between 2014 and 2018. Flows only resumed late last year. The one-year extension postpones but doesn't resolve the longer-term uncertainty around Iraq's export infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ceyhan pipeline extension secures Iraq's northern export route — and Turkey's leverage over it — for another year. As long as Hormuz remains disrupted, Turkey holds a critical chokepoint in global oil logistics.

sections.heading: Economy: TL Deposits Hit 11-Year High as FX Deposits Decline
sections.body:
Finance Minister Mehmet Şimşek announced that the share of Turkish lira deposits in the banking sector has reached 62%, the highest level in 11 years. The announcement came as Central Bank data showed total banking sector deposits declining by 261.9 billion lira in the week ending July 3, while FX deposits fell by $3.25 billion — suggesting a continued shift from foreign currency into lira-denominated accounts.

The Central Bank's total reserves increased by $10.5 billion in the same period, indicating continued reserve accumulation. Banking sector loan volume surpassed 26 trillion lira.

Şimşek characterized the deposit shift as evidence of economic program resilience 'despite multiple shocks' — a reference to the Iran war, Hormuz disruptions, and global market turbulence.
sections.whyItMatters: The 62% TL deposit share is a tangible metric of Şimşek's program gaining traction — dedollarization was a core policy goal. But with the Iran war still disrupting energy prices and the lira under structural pressure, the question is whether this shift holds once external shocks intensify.

sections.heading: LGBTQ Cruise Denied Entry to Turkey, Then Egypt
sections.body:
An LGBTQ cruise operated by Atlantis Events aboard Virgin Voyages' Scarlet Lady was denied entry to the Turkish port of Kuşadası on July 3, with authorities in Aydın province citing 'groups known for behavior incompatible with our society's structure and moral values.' The 10-night Mediterranean cruise then re-routed to Alexandria, Egypt — only to be denied entry again just four hours before arrival on July 9, with no explanation given.

Atlantis Events CEO Rich Campbell noted the company had visited Egypt 'at least five times in the past' without incident, including with 2,500 guests the previous year. Actress Patti LuPone, who was scheduled to perform onboard, wrote on Instagram: 'A ship — a magnificent ship — full of gay men. And me. Denied entry to Turkey simply because of who is on board.'

Turkey's tourism ministry did not respond to requests for comment. The incident drew international media attention during the same week Erdogan hosted 32 NATO leaders.
sections.whyItMatters: The dual denial — first by Turkey, then by Egypt — underscores how LGBTQ rights have become a diplomatic fault line, and the timing was particularly stark: Turkey celebrating its strategic importance to the Western alliance while turning away visitors based on sexual orientation.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara Summit was Turkey's moment to reposition itself at the center of Western defense — and it largely succeeded. Trump's pledge to lift CAATSA sanctions, the emerging S-400-to-Gulf transfer plan, and the $700M KAAN engine sale all signal a decisive thaw in US-Turkey defense relations. But the F-35 remains blocked by Congress, and Israel and Greece will fight Turkey's return to the program. The bigger story is the exposure of how far Turkey went to block a Kurdish invasion of Iran — prioritizing its own security calculus over an allied military operation. Combined with escalating Israel-Turkey tensions over Syria and Lebanon, and the Ceyhan pipeline extension that gives Ankara continued leverage over Iraqi oil exports, Turkey is consolidating power across multiple fronts. The question is whether its balancing act — between NATO, Russia, Iran, and an increasingly hostile Israel — is sustainable or overleveraged.
sources: Forbes, CSMonitor, Hürriyet (via News.am), Middle East Eye, CNN, Reuters, OilPrice, USA Today, Euro2day, Bloomberg HT, Anadolu Agency, Haber7, Dunya, i24News, AP News, The Hill, MarineLink, DevDiscourse, Al Jazeera, New York Times
